ref.title: Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics
ref.author: Ruth Lewin Sime

portrait.image_attribution: C. A. Briggs, “Lise Meitner (1878-1968), lecturing at Catholic University, Washington, D.C., 1946”, Public domain (Smithsonian Institution Archives via Wikimedia Commons)

feed_card.name: Lise Meitner
feed_card.one_line: Austrian physicist who interpreted the experiment that proved uranium nuclei could split, then refused to work on the bomb her theory made possible.
teasers: Her lab partner won the Nobel for the discovery she explained.
After the war she wrote Hahn a letter she never sent.
Denied the Nobel, she ended up on the periodic table instead.

sections.content: Lise Meitner was an Austrian-born physicist who spent thirty years building nuclear physics in Berlin, fled Nazi Germany in 1938 at age 59, and months later worked out the calculation showing that a uranium nucleus can split in two. The Nobel Prize for that discovery went to her chemist collaborator.

content.image_caption: Lise Meitner and Otto Hahn in their laboratory at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry in Berlin, about 1913. Their partnership lasted three decades.
content.image_attribution: Science Service, “Lise Meitner and Otto Hahn in their laboratory, Kaiser-Wilhelm Institut fur Chemie, Berlin”, Public domain (U.S. National Archives via Wikimedia Commons)

content.quote: I will have nothing to do with a bomb.
You must not blame us scientists for the use which war technicians have put our discoveries.
I love physics with all my heart. It is a kind of personal love, as one has for a person to whom one is grateful for many things.

content.known_for: Theoretical interpretation of nuclear fission; co-discovery of protactinium

sections.content: A chemist in Berlin had a result he could not explain, and he wrote to the one physicist he trusted to make sense of it. In December 1938 Otto Hahn, Meitner's partner of thirty years, reported that bombarding uranium with neutrons left behind barium, an element about half the weight of uranium. That was supposed to be impossible. Meitner, by then in exile in Sweden, worked it out with her nephew Otto Frisch. The uranium nucleus had broken in two, and the lost mass had turned into energy, in the amount Einstein's equation predicted. Hahn had done the chemistry. The physics was hers. Her interpretation is the physics under both nuclear power and the bomb, and for decades her name was left off it. When the 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded for the discovery, it went to Hahn alone.

content.title: Locked Out of the Gymnasium, She Studies at Home
content.body: For most of Meitner's youth, Vienna did not admit women to the Gymnasium at all. Her primary schooling ended at 14. Years of private study at her father's expense followed. By the time she could legally begin, her male contemporaries had a decade of laboratory work behind them. She sat the Matura and entered the University of Vienna to read physics. Her doctorate, earned in 1906, made her the second woman in the university's history to do so.

content.title: She Talks Her Way Into Planck's Berlin
content.body: Max Planck did not, as a rule, admit women to his lectures. He made an exception for Meitner, who arrived in Berlin in 1907 and was his assistant within a year. Her footing there was never secure: the institute kept her out of its main laboratory, unsure what to do with a woman doing physics. At the Chemical Institute she met a young chemist named Otto Hahn, and the two began a partnership that would run until 1938.

content.title: The War Teaches Her What Weapons Do
content.body: When the First World War broke out, Meitner volunteered as an X-ray nurse for the Austrian army and was sent to military hospitals near the front. No one had trained her on the equipment, so she trained herself. For a stretch of months she handled shrapnel wounds and amputations, then returned to Berlin in late 1916. In her own account, those months near the front were what turned her against weapons.

content.title: The Racial Laws Erase What She Built
content.body: The University of Berlin accepted her Habilitation in 1922. In 1926 she was appointed Extraordinary Professor of Physics, an early instance of a woman holding a physics professorship in Germany. By the early 1930s she headed the physics section of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry and ran her own group on radioactive elements. Then the racial laws arrived, and the standing she had spent two decades earning counted for nothing.
content.image_caption: The Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry in Berlin-Dahlem, where Meitner led the physics section, photographed shortly after the building opened in 1911.
content.image_attribution: Unknown author, “Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry, Dahlem, 1911”, Public domain (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: She Leaves Germany With Almost Nothing
content.body: The Anschluss in March 1938 turned Austrian Jews into German citizens overnight and stripped away the protection her Austrian passport had given her. Friends had urged her to go. She stayed, hoping a post abroad would come through, until staying became dangerous. In July, with the help of colleagues, she crossed into the Netherlands carrying little more than what she could pack, and would not return for ten years.

content.title: A Letter From Hahn, a Walk in the Snow
content.body: Meitner spent her first Christmas of exile in Sweden, where Otto Frisch came to join her. In her pocket was a letter from Hahn: he and Strassmann had found barium where barium should not be, and he wanted to know what the physics had to say. They did the work outdoors, on a walk through the snow near Kungalv. Frisch went on skis, Meitner on foot, and when the problem took hold they stopped to do the arithmetic. The nucleus, they reasoned, could stretch and split into two, and the mass that went missing in the split should reappear as about 200 million electron-volts of energy per atom, exactly the figure Einstein's equation gave. That was what Hahn had seen, without knowing it.

content.title: Her Answer to the Bomb Is No
content.body: Frisch named the process “fission” in the paper the two of them published in Nature in February 1939. The split she had explained released energy, but it also threw off spare neutrons, each able to split a further nucleus and keep the reaction running on its own. That was the self-sustaining chain reaction that made a bomb physically conceivable, and the energy figure she had first calculated soon became the premise of weapons programs in several countries. When the invitation came for Meitner to join that work, her answer was blunt: “I will have nothing to do with a bomb.” She spent the war in Stockholm, where she had little support and went largely unrecognized. The news of Hiroshima reached her in August 1945, and she said nothing to the press.

content.title: The Liquid-Drop Interpretation of Nuclear Fission
content.body: Before Meitner and Frisch, splitting a heavy nucleus into two pieces of nearly equal size was thought to be impossible. The picture she reached for was a drop of liquid: surface tension pulling the nucleus together, electrostatic repulsion pushing it apart. In uranium those two forces very nearly cancel. That near-balance is the whole point. With the forces so finely matched, a single neutron is enough to set the drop wobbling, stretching, pinching at the middle, until it breaks into two smaller drops. Then she counted the cost in mass. The two fragments together weigh measurably less than the uranium nucleus and the neutron that struck it, and by E = mc² that missing mass has to come out as energy, roughly 200 million electron-volts of it, flung outward as the pieces fly apart. Frisch confirmed the prediction in the laboratory within weeks. The paper that explained it, dated 11 February 1939, was two pages long.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 248' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.75'><circle cx='66' cy='96' r='34'/><circle cx='300' cy='74' r='22'/><circle cx='342' cy='118' r='24'/></g><ellipse cx='188' cy='96' rx='46' ry='24' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M108 96 h24'/><path d='M132 96 l-9 -5 m9 5 l-9 5'/><path d='M242 96 h22'/><path d='M264 96 l-9 -5 m9 5 l-9 5'/></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'><text x='66' y='101'>U</text><text x='300' y='79'>Ba</text><text x='342' y='123'>Kr</text></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle' opacity='0.8'><text x='66' y='154'>uranium</text><text x='188' y='154' fill='var(--accent)'>splits</text><text x='321' y='168'>fragments</text></g><text x='200' y='216' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>about 200 MeV</text></svg>

sections.content: Reserved, she was slow to claim anything for herself. She kept the small things the same way: a turned-down honorary degree she felt she had not earned, a daily hour at the piano, decades of letters written in German long after Sweden had become home. With Hahn the warmth held for thirty years and then broke. It broke partly because he kept publishing the fission work without her name on it, and partly because of what he wrote to her after the war, that ordinary Germans had suffered as much as the people the regime murdered. She answered him in a letter that she is reported never to have sent: he and his colleagues had worked for Nazi Germany and put up no resistance, while millions were killed and no one protested.

content.body: For years after the war her part was left out. Hahn took the 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery of fission and collected the medal in person in 1946, and the histories written in the 1950s reduced Meitner to his assistant. What turned it around was a book: Ruth Lewin Sime's 1996 biography *Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics*, built on the Hahn-Meitner letters held at the Churchill Archives Centre in Cambridge. Letter by letter, Sime showed that the physical interpretation of fission had come from Meitner. The account most physicists give today splits the credit the way the work actually divided, Hahn and Strassmann on the chemistry, Meitner and Frisch on the physics. The Nobel committee has never reopened the question.
content.present_day_impact: Element 109 was named meitnerium in 1997, 29 years after her death. The Hahn-Meitner Institute in Berlin, founded in 1959, carried both their names from the start, and both of them came to its dedication.

content.question: What was Meitner's specific contribution to the discovery of nuclear fission in December 1938?
options: She ran the chemistry experiment that detected barium in uranium residue
She supplied the physical interpretation that explained Hahn's chemical result
She built the neutron source used in Berlin
She first proposed that uranium could be split, before any data existed
content.explanation: Hahn and Strassmann did the chemistry in Berlin and detected the barium. Meitner, in exile, used the liquid-drop model and Einstein's equation to explain why barium appeared and to estimate the energy released. The chemistry alone could not say what had physically happened to the nucleus.

content.question: Why did Meitner lose her protection from the Nazi racial laws in 1938?
options: The racial laws classified Jewishness by ancestry, and the Anschluss removed the shield of her Austrian citizenship
Her academic title was formally revoked that year
She had given up her citizenship years earlier
She was expelled from the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute by a court order
content.explanation: The Nazi racial laws defined Jewishness by descent, not religion. Meitner's Austrian citizenship had partly shielded her until March 1938, when Germany annexed Austria and she became subject to those laws.

content.question: What model of the atomic nucleus did Meitner use to explain fission?
options: The plum-pudding model
The Bohr planetary model with electron shells
The liquid-drop model, treating the nucleus as a charged droplet
A quark-based model derived from the Standard Model
content.explanation: The liquid-drop model treats the nucleus as a drop held together by surface tension and pushed apart by electrostatic repulsion. For uranium those forces nearly balance, so a small disturbance can elongate the drop and split it in two. The Standard Model and quarks were formulated decades later.

content.question: Why did the 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry go to Otto Hahn alone, with no share for Meitner?
options: She had not contributed to the original Berlin experiments
The Nobel committee judged that exiled scientists could not be considered
A combination of disciplinary boundaries, wartime isolation, and her position as an exiled physicist
She declined the prize in writing
content.explanation: Historians of science point to several factors. The Chemistry committee defined the discovery as chemistry, Meitner was in exile and largely invisible to the Stockholm committee, and the work was published separately. The omission has been studied as a clear case of Nobel misattribution.

content.question: Roughly how much energy did Meitner calculate would be released per uranium atom split?
options: About 1 electron-volt
About 200 electron-volts
About 200 million electron-volts
About 200 billion electron-volts
content.explanation: The mass difference between the uranium nucleus and the two fragments, multiplied by c squared via Einstein's equation, gives roughly 200 million electron-volts per fission, far more than chemical reactions release per atom. Frisch confirmed this experimentally weeks later.

content.question: How did Meitner respond when invited to join the work to build an atomic bomb?
options: She accepted but withdrew when she saw the lab conditions
She agreed only to advisory work, not weapons design
She refused, saying she would have nothing to do with a bomb
She demanded that Hahn join her as a condition
content.explanation: Meitner had seen the wounds of the First World War as a front-line X-ray nurse, and her opposition to weapons work was settled long before. Her refusal is recorded in Sime's biography.

content.question: What present-day form of recognition did Meitner not live to see?
options: A posthumous Nobel Prize awarded in 1971
Element 109 was named meitnerium in 1997
The Hahn-Meitner Institute was first founded under that name in 1990
A Royal Society medal given in her name in 1980
content.explanation: Meitnerium, atomic number 109, was officially named for Meitner in 1997, 29 years after her death. The Hahn-Meitner Institute had already been named for both Meitner and Hahn in 1959, while she was alive. There is no posthumous Nobel.

sections.content: The record holds her hesitations next to her work. The hardest is that she stayed in Berlin too long. She went on working at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute while Jewish colleagues were pushed out, and she did not protest the dismissals in public. There is the question of Hahn as well: she kept the working relationship alive by letter even after he left her name off the discovery. Her own silence, she wrote in private after the war, was the thing she could not forgive in herself.

content.label: Sime RL. Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics. University of California Press, 1996.
Meitner L, Frisch OR. Disintegration of Uranium by Neutrons: A New Type of Nuclear Reaction. Nature 1939;143:239-240.
Hahn O, Strassmann F. Uber den Nachweis und das Verhalten der bei der Bestrahlung des Urans mittels Neutronen entstehenden Erdalkalimetalle. Naturwissenschaften 1939;27:11-15.
The Papers of Lise Meitner, Churchill Archives Centre, Cambridge (MTNR collection)
Frisch OR. Lise Meitner, 1878-1968. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the Royal Society 1970;16:405-420.
American Physical Society: December 1938, Discovery of Nuclear Fission
